questions.question: Which of the following is a benzylic halide?
questions.explanation: In C6H5CH2Cl, chlorine is attached to an sp3 carbon directly adjacent to a benzene ring, so it is a benzylic halide.

questions.question: The correct IUPAC name of CH3CH(Cl)CH2CH3 is:
questions.explanation: The longest chain contains four carbon atoms and chlorine receives the lowest possible locant, 2.

questions.question: Which statement correctly explains the polar nature of the C–Cl bond in chloroethane?
options: Carbon is more electronegative than chlorine.
Chlorine completely accepts an electron from carbon.
Chlorine is more electronegative and pulls the shared electron pair towards itself.
The C–Cl bond contains two lone pairs shared equally.
Chloroethane contains C+ and Cl− ions.
questions.explanation: The difference in electronegativity creates Cδ+–Clδ−, making the bond polar covalent rather than ionic.

questions.question: An alcohol is to be converted into an alkyl chloride. Which reagent is generally preferred when a pure alkyl chloride is desired because the inorganic by-products escape as gases?
questions.explanation: SOCl2 converts alcohols into alkyl chlorides while SO2 and HCl escape as gases, simplifying purification.

questions.question: A student wants to convert C2H5Br into C2H5I. Which combination is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: NaI in dry acetone is used in the Finkelstein reaction to convert alkyl bromides or chlorides into alkyl iodides.

questions.question: Consider CH3Br and (CH3)3CBr reacting separately with a strong nucleophile in a polar aprotic solvent. Which statement is most appropriate?
options: (CH3)3CBr undergoes SN2 faster because it forms a stable carbocation.
CH3Br undergoes SN2 faster because backside attack is least hindered.
Both react at the same rate because the C–Br bond is identical.
(CH3)3CBr undergoes SN2 faster because tertiary carbon is more electron-rich.
questions.explanation: A strong nucleophile and polar aprotic solvent favour SN2. Methyl bromide has minimal steric hindrance, whereas backside attack at a tertiary carbon is strongly hindered.

questions.question: A chiral alkyl halide undergoes substitution through a planar carbocation intermediate. Which observation is most consistent with this mechanism?
options: Attack can occur from both sides, leading to racemisation.
The reaction cannot involve a nucleophile.
questions.explanation: A planar carbocation is characteristic of SN1. The nucleophile can attack from either side, producing racemisation.

questions.question: CH3CH2Br is treated separately with aqueous KOH and hot alcoholic KOH. Which pair of major products is expected respectively?
questions.explanation: Aqueous KOH mainly favours nucleophilic substitution to form ethanol, while hot alcoholic KOH favours β-elimination to form ethene.

questions.question: A compound CH3CHBrCH2CH3 is heated with alcoholic KOH. Two alkenes can form. Why is but-2-ene expected as the major product?
options: The halogen must always leave from carbon-2.
But-2-ene has fewer carbon atoms around the double bond.
But-2-ene is the more substituted and generally more stable alkene.
But-1-ene cannot be formed because it has a terminal double bond.
questions.explanation: According to Zaitsev's rule, the more substituted alkene is generally the major product. But-2-ene is more substituted than but-1-ene.

questions.question: An alkyl bromide gives a nitrile with KCN but an isocyanide with AgCN. Which explanation best accounts for the different products?
options: KCN contains carbon but AgCN contains no carbon.
AgCN converts the alkyl bromide into a carbocation before substitution.
CN− can bond only through nitrogen in ionic compounds.
KCN is mainly ionic and provides carbon-end attack, whereas covalent AgCN favours nitrogen-end attack.
Silver changes the alkyl group into an aryl group.
questions.explanation: CN− is an ambident nucleophile. Ionic KCN allows attack mainly through carbon to form R–CN, while covalent AgCN favours attack through nitrogen to form R–NC.

questions.question: CH3CH2Br reacts separately with KNO2 and AgNO2. Which pair of products is formed respectively?
questions.explanation: KNO2 is ionic and favours oxygen-end attack to form an alkyl nitrite, R–ONO. AgNO2 favours nitrogen-end attack to form a nitroalkane, R–NO2.

questions.question: Which combination of substrate, nucleophile and solvent is expected to favour SN2 most strongly?
questions.explanation: SN2 is favoured by an unhindered methyl substrate, a strong nucleophile and a polar aprotic solvent such as DMSO.

questions.question: A tertiary alkyl halide is placed in a polar protic solvent with a weak nucleophile. Why can substitution still occur readily?
options: The weak nucleophile directly attacks the crowded carbon by SN2.
The solvent converts the tertiary carbon into a primary carbon.
A stable tertiary carbocation can form before nucleophilic attack.
The halogen becomes a strong nucleophile and attacks the carbon.
Polar protic solvents prevent ion formation.
questions.explanation: These conditions favour SN1. The rate-determining step is formation of a relatively stable tertiary carbocation, so a strong nucleophile is not essential.

questions.question: Two reactions use the same alkyl halide. Reaction I doubles the nucleophile concentration, while Reaction II proceeds through carbocation formation. Which statement is correct?
options: The rate of both reactions must double.
Only the SN1 reaction rate doubles.
The rate of the carbocation-forming reaction is directly proportional to nucleophile concentration.
Doubling nucleophile concentration can increase the SN2 rate but does not directly affect the SN1 rate.
Nucleophile concentration never affects substitution reactions.
questions.explanation: SN2 rate depends on both substrate and nucleophile concentrations. SN1 rate depends only on substrate concentration because carbocation formation is the slow step.

questions.question: Which statement correctly compares the transition state of SN2 with the intermediate of SN1?
options: Both are stable species that can be isolated.
SN2 has a carbocation intermediate, while SN1 has only a transition state.
SN2 has a high-energy transition state but no intermediate, whereas SN1 forms a carbocation intermediate.
Both mechanisms contain a carbanion intermediate.
SN1 and SN2 are both one-step reactions.
questions.explanation: SN2 is a concerted one-step mechanism with a transition state. SN1 occurs in steps and forms a carbocation intermediate.

questions.question: A student claims that every molecule containing a carbon attached to three different groups is chiral. Why is this statement incorrect?
options: A chiral carbon must be attached to four different groups.
Chiral carbons must contain a double bond.
Only carbon attached to halogen can be chiral.
Three different groups always produce racemisation.
questions.explanation: For the simple tetrahedral carbon cases studied here, a chiral carbon must be bonded to four different groups.

questions.question: Which compound contains a chiral carbon atom?
questions.explanation: In CH3CHBrCH2CH3, the carbon bearing Br is attached to H, Br, CH3 and C2H5, four different groups.

questions.question: A chiral alkyl halide undergoes SN2 substitution. The nucleophile attacks from the side opposite to the leaving group. What is the expected stereochemical result?
options: Racemisation because a planar carbocation forms
Retention because the nucleophile follows the leaving group
No change because configuration is unrelated to mechanism
Inversion of configuration due to backside attack
Formation of equal amounts of alkene and alcohol
questions.explanation: Backside attack in SN2 reverses the spatial arrangement around the chiral carbon, producing inversion of configuration.

questions.question: Why does a polar protic solvent generally favour SN1 over SN2 when the substrate can form a stable carbocation?
options: It prevents the leaving group from departing.
It stabilises ions, including the carbocation formed during SN1.
It converts all nucleophiles into electrophiles.
It removes every beta-hydrogen from the substrate.
It makes tertiary substrates less crowded.
questions.explanation: Polar protic solvents stabilise the carbocation and leaving-group ions, helping ionisation and therefore favouring SN1.

questions.question: OH− is both a nucleophile and a base. Which condition most strongly shifts its role towards beta-elimination in the reactions studied in this chapter?
options: Aqueous medium at ordinary conditions with an easily attacked carbon
Hot alcoholic KOH with a substrate containing a beta-hydrogen
Polar aprotic solvent with methyl bromide
questions.explanation: Hot alcoholic KOH favours the basic behaviour of OH− and beta-hydrogen abstraction. If a suitable beta-hydrogen is present, elimination becomes favourable.

questions.question: Which alkyl halide cannot undergo beta-elimination with hot alcoholic KOH?
questions.explanation: CH3Br has no beta-carbon and therefore no beta-hydrogen. Hence normal beta-elimination cannot occur.

questions.question: CH3CHBrCH2CH3 is heated with alcoholic KOH. Which statement best explains the formation of but-2-ene as the major product?
options: The base removes hydrogen only from terminal carbon atoms.
The C–Br bond can break only towards carbon-3.
But-2-ene is the more substituted and more stable alkene.
But-1-ene cannot form because it contains a double bond.
Alcoholic KOH follows the anti-Markovnikov rule.
questions.explanation: According to Zaitsev's rule, the more substituted alkene is generally formed as the major product. But-2-ene is more substituted than but-1-ene.

questions.question: An alkyl halide is heated with alcoholic KOH. A student predicts that elimination must always be the major pathway. Which factor most directly shows why this prediction may fail?
options: The alkyl halide may lack a beta-hydrogen required for alkene formation.
Alcohol always converts OH− completely into a weak nucleophile.
Heating prevents carbon-halogen bond cleavage in primary halides.
Elimination requires the formation of a carbocation in every case.
Alcoholic KOH can remove hydrogen only from the carbon bearing the halogen.
questions.explanation: Beta-elimination requires removal of a beta-hydrogen from a carbon adjacent to the carbon bearing the halogen. If no suitable beta-hydrogen is present, normal beta-elimination cannot occur even under alcoholic KOH and heating.

questions.question: Which sequence correctly describes a concerted E2 elimination?
options: Leaving group departs, carbocation forms, then beta-hydrogen is removed.
Nucleophile attacks carbon, then a double bond forms.
Base removes a beta-hydrogen while C=C forms and the leaving group departs in the same step.
A carbanion forms first and remains as a stable intermediate.
The halogen attacks a beta-hydrogen and forms HX.
questions.explanation: In E2 elimination, beta-hydrogen abstraction, double-bond formation and leaving-group departure occur together in one concerted step.

questions.question: CH3CH2Br reacts with Mg in dry ether to form compound X. X is then treated with water. What is the final organic product?
questions.explanation: CH3CH2Br first forms CH3CH2MgBr. Grignard reagents react with water to form the corresponding hydrocarbon, so ethane is produced.

questions.question: Why must the ether used in Grignard reagent preparation be anhydrous?
options: Water reacts with RMgX and converts the R group into the corresponding hydrocarbon.
Water increases the rate of Wurtz reaction.
Water converts alkyl halides into haloarenes.
Water prevents ether from dissolving the alkyl halide.
questions.explanation: Grignard reagents are destroyed by proton sources such as water. RMgX reacts with H2O to give RH.

questions.question: Dry ether is especially suitable for Grignard reagent formation because:
options: It provides OH− ions for the reaction.
It acts as a strong acid.
It does not contain water and its oxygen lone pairs help stabilise magnesium by coordination.
It converts Mg into Mg2+ in aqueous solution.
questions.explanation: Dry ether avoids destruction of RMgX by moisture, while ether oxygen can coordinate with magnesium and stabilise the Grignard reagent.

questions.question: A student performs a Wurtz reaction using CH3Br and C2H5Br together. Why is this method unsuitable for preparing pure propane?
options: Propane cannot be formed in a Wurtz reaction.
Sodium reacts only with methyl bromide.
A mixture containing ethane, propane and butane can form.
Dry ether prevents coupling of different alkyl groups.
questions.explanation: Using two different alkyl halides allows R–R, R–R' and R'–R' coupling, producing a mixture of alkanes.

questions.question: Which alkyl halide should be used in a Wurtz reaction to prepare n-butane as a single major coupling product?
questions.explanation: Two ethyl groups from CH3CH2Br couple in the Wurtz reaction to form CH3CH2CH2CH3, n-butane.

questions.question: Chlorobenzene and chloroethane both contain a C–Cl bond. Why is chlorobenzene much less reactive towards normal nucleophilic substitution?
options: Chlorobenzene contains an ionic C–Cl bond.
The chlorine atom in chlorobenzene has no lone pairs.
The C–Cl bond in chlorobenzene gains partial double-bond character by resonance and is attached to an sp2 carbon.
Chloroethane cannot undergo SN1 or SN2 reactions.
The benzene ring converts chlorine into a positively charged ion.
questions.explanation: Resonance gives the C–Cl bond partial double-bond character, and the sp2 carbon forms a shorter, stronger bond than an sp3 carbon. Both effects reduce nucleophilic substitution reactivity.

questions.question: Which statement best explains why chlorobenzene does not readily undergo an SN1 reaction?
options: Chlorine cannot act as a leaving group in any compound.
Formation of a phenyl carbocation would be highly unstable.
The benzene ring contains no pi electrons.
SN1 reactions occur only with alkyl iodides.
questions.explanation: SN1 would require formation of a phenyl carbocation, which is highly unstable. Therefore chlorobenzene does not readily follow the SN1 pathway.

questions.question: Which statement best explains why chlorobenzene does not readily undergo an SN2 reaction?
options: The benzene ring hinders normal backside attack at the sp2 carbon bonded to chlorine.
The carbon bonded to chlorine is sp3 hybridised.
A stable carbocation forms before nucleophilic attack.
The C–Cl bond is purely ionic.
questions.explanation: Normal backside attack at the sp2 carbon of chlorobenzene is difficult because of the benzene ring and its electron cloud. The strong C–Cl bond also makes SN2 difficult.

questions.question: Consider chlorobenzene, m-nitrochlorobenzene and p-nitrochlorobenzene. Which is the correct increasing order of ease of nucleophilic substitution of chlorine?
questions.explanation: The key NCERT concept is that NO₂ at ortho/para strongly increases reactivity through resonance stabilisation, while meta-NO₂ does not provide this resonance stabilisation.So Chlorobenzene ~ m-nitrochlorobenzene

questions.question: Why does a nitro group at the para position strongly increase nucleophilic substitution in chlorobenzene?
options: It forms a stable phenyl carbocation.
It donates electrons to chlorine by the +I effect.
It stabilises the negatively charged intermediate through resonance.
It prevents the C–Cl bond from becoming polar.
questions.explanation: A para-NO2 group withdraws electron density and stabilises the negatively charged intermediate formed during nucleophilic aromatic substitution through resonance.

questions.question: Why does a meta-NO2 group not significantly increase nucleophilic substitution of chlorobenzene, unlike an ortho- or para-NO2 group?
options: A meta-NO2 group is electron donating.
A meta-NO2 group cannot effectively stabilise the negatively charged intermediate by resonance.
The meta position contains no pi electrons.
A meta-NO2 group removes chlorine before substitution.
Nitro groups show a +I effect only at the meta position.
questions.explanation: At the meta position, the NO2 group cannot directly stabilise the negative charge of the nucleophilic substitution intermediate through resonance.

questions.question: Which order correctly represents reactivity towards electrophilic substitution?
questions.explanation: Benzene is more reactive than chlorobenzene because chlorine deactivates the ring by its -I effect. The strongly electron-withdrawing NO2 group further deactivates p-nitrochlorobenzene.

questions.question: The same chlorine atom in chlorobenzene deactivates the ring but directs electrophiles to ortho and para positions. Which explanation is correct?
options: Its +I effect deactivates the ring and -R effect directs substitution.
Its -I effect lowers overall ring electron density, while lone-pair donation by resonance increases relative electron density at ortho and para positions.
Chlorine first leaves the ring and then returns to the ortho position.
The inductive and resonance effects of chlorine are identical.
Chlorine activates all positions equally through hyperconjugation.
questions.explanation: The -I effect of chlorine causes overall deactivation. Its lone-pair resonance donation increases electron density relatively at ortho and para positions, making chlorine ortho-para directing.

questions.question: Chlorobenzene undergoes nitration. Which pair of products is mainly expected?
questions.explanation: Chlorine is an ortho-para directing group, so nitration mainly produces ortho- and para-nitrochlorobenzene.

questions.question: Which electrophile attacks the aromatic ring during nitration?
questions.explanation: The nitronium ion, NO2+, is the electrophile responsible for nitration of the aromatic ring.

questions.question: During chlorination of chlorobenzene, why is anhydrous FeCl3 used with Cl2?
options: It supplies Cl− as a strong nucleophile.
It activates and polarises Cl2, producing electrophilic chlorine character.
It removes all pi electrons from the benzene ring.
It acts as a reducing agent.
questions.explanation: FeCl3 acts as a Lewis acid and activates Cl2. This produces strongly polarised chlorine with electrophilic character that can attack the aromatic ring.

questions.question: Chlorobenzene undergoes sulphonation on heating with concentrated H2SO4. Which products are mainly formed?
questions.explanation: Chlorine is ortho-para directing, so sulphonation mainly occurs at the ortho and para positions.

questions.question: Chlorobenzene undergoes halogenation, nitration and sulphonation mainly at ortho and para positions. What common factor controls this orientation?
options: Chlorine donates its lone pair into the ring by resonance, increasing relative electron density at ortho and para positions.
The C–Cl bond breaks before every electrophilic substitution.
The meta positions contain no hydrogen atoms.
questions.explanation: Although chlorine deactivates the ring by its -I effect, lone-pair donation through resonance increases relative electron density at the ortho and para positions.

questions.question: Which pair correctly matches an aromatic substitution reaction with its attacking electrophile or electrophilic species?
questions.explanation: Sulphonation involves electrophilic SO3 or its protonated form. NO2+ is used in nitration, and activated chlorine has electrophilic character in halogenation.

questions.question: In the electrophilic substitution reactions of chlorobenzene, the para product is often major over the ortho product. Which explanation is most appropriate?
options: The para position has no resonance stabilisation.
Chlorine directs only to the para position.
Attack at the para position generally suffers less steric hindrance.
The para carbon carries a permanent negative charge.
questions.explanation: Both ortho and para positions are activated relative to meta by resonance, but para attack is generally less sterically hindered.

questions.question: Chlorobenzene is treated with CH3Cl in the presence of anhydrous AlCl3. In the simplified reaction pattern studied in this chapter, which major product is expected?
questions.explanation: In the simplified electrophilic substitution pattern, chlorine directs the incoming methyl group to ortho and para positions, with the para product generally favoured due to lower steric hindrance.

questions.question: Which reagent combination represents Friedel-Crafts acylation in the reaction patterns studied here?
questions.explanation: An acyl chloride such as CH3COCl with anhydrous AlCl3 is used for Friedel-Crafts acylation.

questions.question: CH3COCl is used in a Friedel-Crafts reaction. Which group is introduced into the aromatic ring?
questions.explanation: CH3COCl is ethanoyl chloride (acetyl chloride) and introduces the acyl group -COCH3.

questions.question: Which comparison correctly distinguishes Wurtz, Fittig and Wurtz-Fittig reactions?
options: Wurtz: Ar-Ar; Fittig: R-R; Wurtz-Fittig: R-OH
Wurtz: R-R; Fittig: Ar-Ar; Wurtz-Fittig: Ar-R
Wurtz: Ar-R; Fittig: R-R; Wurtz-Fittig: Ar-Ar
All three are nucleophilic substitution reactions with OH−.
questions.explanation: Wurtz couples two alkyl groups, Fittig couples two aryl groups, and Wurtz-Fittig couples an aryl group with an alkyl group.

questions.question: Chlorobenzene and CH3Cl are heated with sodium in dry ether. Which product and reaction name are correctly matched?
questions.explanation: An aryl halide and an alkyl halide couple with sodium in dry ether to form an alkyl arene. Chlorobenzene and CH3Cl form toluene by the Wurtz-Fittig reaction.

questions.question: Two molecules of chlorobenzene are treated with sodium in dry ether. Which conclusion is correct?
questions.explanation: Fittig reaction couples two aryl halide molecules using sodium in dry ether to form a biaryl compound such as biphenyl.

questions.question: Which compound is commonly known as methylene chloride?
questions.explanation: Dichloromethane, CH2Cl2, is commonly known as methylene chloride.

questions.question: Chloroform is stored in closed, dark-coloured bottles mainly because exposure to air and light can produce:
questions.explanation: Chloroform is slowly oxidised by oxygen in the presence of light to form highly poisonous phosgene, COCl2.

questions.question: A bottle of CHCl3 is left partly filled and exposed to sunlight for a long time. Which reaction is the main safety concern?
options: CHCl3 reacts with nitrogen to form HCN.
CHCl3 is oxidised by oxygen to poisonous COCl2.
CHCl3 forms CCl4 by losing hydrogen.
questions.explanation: Air provides oxygen and light promotes oxidation of chloroform to phosgene. This explains why chloroform is protected from air and light.

questions.question: The antiseptic action associated with iodoform is mainly due to:
questions.explanation: The antiseptic property associated with iodoform is due to the release of free iodine rather than iodoform itself.

questions.question: Which statement correctly compares chloroform and iodoform?
options: Chloroform is CH2Cl2 and iodoform is CHI3.
Chloroform can form poisonous phosgene on oxidation, while iodoform is associated with antiseptic action due to free iodine.
Both are mainly responsible for ozone depletion.
questions.explanation: CHCl3 can oxidise to phosgene, whereas CHI3 has been used as an antiseptic because of liberated iodine.

questions.question: Carbon tetrachloride was formerly used in fire extinguishers and as a cleaning solvent. Why has its use been restricted?
options: It is toxic and also contributes to ozone layer depletion.
questions.explanation: CCl4 is toxic, particularly to the liver, and its release into the atmosphere contributes to ozone depletion.

questions.question: Freon-12 has the formula CCl2F2. Which property originally made compounds like Freons useful as refrigerants?
options: They are highly reactive with metals.
They are relatively stable, non-corrosive and easily liquefied.
They rapidly react with ozone near the Earth's surface.
They form ionic solutions in water.
questions.explanation: The stability, low reactivity, non-corrosive nature and ease of liquefaction of Freons made them suitable refrigerants.

questions.question: The same chemical stability that made CFCs useful also creates an environmental problem. Which explanation best connects these facts?
options: Their stability allows them to persist and reach the stratosphere, where chlorine radicals can participate in ozone destruction.
Their stability makes them react instantly with water.
Stable CFCs absorb all oxygen from the atmosphere.
Their stability converts them directly into DDT.
Stable CFCs behave as strong bases in the troposphere.
questions.explanation: Because CFCs are stable in the lower atmosphere, they can survive long enough to reach the stratosphere. There, photochemical processes release chlorine radicals involved in ozone destruction.

questions.question: Why does DDT tend to accumulate in the fatty tissues of organisms?
options: It is highly soluble in water.
It is fat-soluble and is metabolised very slowly.
It forms hydrogen bonds with water.
questions.explanation: DDT is fat-soluble, chemically stable and slowly metabolised, so it accumulates in fatty tissues.

questions.question: A small concentration of DDT is present in water. Its concentration becomes progressively higher in small organisms, fish and finally fish-eating birds. This phenomenon is called:
questions.explanation: The increase in concentration of a persistent substance such as DDT at successive trophic levels is called biomagnification.

questions.question: Chlorobenzene is difficult to hydrolyse under ordinary conditions, but p-nitrochlorobenzene undergoes nucleophilic substitution more readily. Which combination of factors best explains these observations?
options: The C–Cl bond in chlorobenzene is weak, while the nitro group strengthens nucleophilic attack by donating electrons.
Resonance gives the C–Cl bond in chlorobenzene partial double-bond character, while a para-NO2 group stabilises the negatively charged substitution intermediate.
Chlorobenzene forms an unstable carbocation, while p-nitrochlorobenzene undergoes an SN1 reaction through a stable carbocation.
The carbon bonded to chlorine in chlorobenzene is sp3 hybridised, while the nitro group converts it into an sp2 carbon.
The nitro group weakens the C–Cl bond by converting chlorine into a positively charged leaving group.
questions.explanation: In chlorobenzene, resonance gives the C–Cl bond partial double-bond character, making bond cleavage difficult. A para-NO2 group increases nucleophilic substitution by stabilising the negatively charged intermediate through resonance.

questions.question: A student compares chlorobenzene with p-nitrochlorobenzene. Which statement correctly describes their behaviour towards electrophilic and nucleophilic substitution?
options: p-Nitrochlorobenzene is more reactive in both reactions.
Chlorobenzene is more reactive in both reactions.
p-Nitrochlorobenzene is more reactive towards electrophilic substitution but less reactive towards nucleophilic substitution.
Chlorobenzene is more reactive towards electrophilic substitution, while p-nitrochlorobenzene is more reactive towards nucleophilic substitution.
questions.explanation: NO2 strongly withdraws electron density, making electrophilic attack more difficult. However, a para-NO2 group stabilises the negatively charged intermediate in nucleophilic aromatic substitution.

questions.question: Which set of conditions and products is correctly matched?
options: CH3CH2Br + Mg/dry ether, followed by H2O → CH3CH3
questions.explanation: Ethyl bromide forms C2H5MgBr with magnesium in dry ether. Hydrolysis of the Grignard reagent gives ethane.

questions.question: An alkyl halide is chiral, forms a relatively stable carbocation and is reacted in a polar protic solvent with a weak nucleophile. Which combination of mechanism and stereochemical result is most likely?
questions.explanation: Stable carbocation formation, polar protic solvent and weak nucleophile favour SN1. The planar carbocation can be attacked from both sides, leading to racemisation.

questions.question: Which statement correctly connects substrate structure, solvent and SN2 reactivity?
options: Tertiary alkyl halides react fastest by SN2 in polar aprotic solvents.
Polar protic solvents always make nucleophiles stronger.
Methyl and primary halides favour SN2 because backside attack is less hindered, and polar aprotic solvents help keep anionic nucleophiles more reactive.
SN2 requires formation of a stable carbocation.
SN2 rate is independent of nucleophile concentration.
questions.explanation: Low steric hindrance favours backside attack, while polar aprotic solvents do not strongly solvate anionic nucleophiles and therefore favour SN2.

questions.question: A compound reacts readily by SN1 and also has beta-hydrogens. On heating under strongly basic conditions, elimination becomes important. Which idea best explains this change in major pathway?
options: Heating permanently converts the substrate into a primary halide.
A strong base can abstract a beta-hydrogen, and heating favours elimination over substitution.
The leaving group becomes a nucleophile and attacks hydrogen.
Heat removes the C–X bond without any other bond changes.
questions.explanation: A substrate may favour SN1 under ionising conditions, but strong basic conditions and heat make beta-hydrogen abstraction and alkene formation competitive or dominant.

questions.question: Which reaction sequence correctly converts an alkyl bromide first into a Grignard reagent and then into a hydrocarbon containing the same number of carbon atoms?
questions.explanation: RBr reacts with magnesium in dry ether to form RMgBr. Water then protonates the carbon bonded to magnesium, producing RH with the same carbon skeleton.

questions.question: A student wants to distinguish conceptually between Fittig and Wurtz-Fittig reactions by examining only the organic reactants. Which observation is sufficient?
options: Fittig uses two aryl halides, whereas Wurtz-Fittig uses an aryl halide and an alkyl halide.
Fittig uses alcohol, whereas Wurtz-Fittig uses water.
Fittig uses magnesium, whereas Wurtz-Fittig uses sodium.
Fittig produces only alkenes, whereas Wurtz-Fittig produces alcohols.
Fittig is SN1, whereas Wurtz-Fittig is SN2.
questions.explanation: Fittig couples two aryl halides to form Ar-Ar. Wurtz-Fittig couples an aryl halide with an alkyl halide to form Ar-R.

questions.question: Which comparison correctly connects the environmental behaviour of Freons and DDT?
options: Both are rapidly decomposed and therefore harmless.
Freons accumulate mainly in fatty tissues, while DDT destroys ozone through chlorine radicals.
The persistence of Freons allows them to reach the stratosphere, while the persistence and fat solubility of DDT allow biomagnification.
Both cause environmental damage only because they are strong acids.
Both are highly soluble in water and rapidly move out of organisms.
questions.explanation: The stability of CFCs allows atmospheric persistence and transport to the stratosphere. DDT is persistent and fat-soluble, causing accumulation and biomagnification in food chains.

questions.question: A student makes four observations: (I) chlorine deactivates a benzene ring towards electrophilic substitution, (II) chlorine directs incoming electrophiles mainly to ortho and para positions, (III) a para-NO2 group increases nucleophilic substitution of chlorine, and (IV) the same para-NO2 group decreases electrophilic substitution. Which option is correct?
options: Only I and II are correct.
Only III and IV are correct.
I, II and III are correct but IV is incorrect.
Only I and IV are correct.
questions.explanation: Chlorine deactivates by its -I effect but directs ortho and para through resonance donation. A para-NO2 group stabilises the negatively charged intermediate in nucleophilic substitution but withdraws electron density and therefore further deactivates the ring towards electrophilic substitution.
